name: performance
context: SQLite performance tuning — PRAGMAs, indexing strategy, EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN, and bulk operation patterns
info: This node covers everything an agent needs to make SQLite fast: connection-time PRAGMAs (journal_mode, synchronous, cache_size, mmap_size, temp_store), indexing strategy (composite, partial, covering, expression indexes), query analysis with EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN, bulk insert optimization (transactions, prepared statements), VACUUM and ANALYZE for maintenance, and common performance antipatterns to avoid. SQLite can handle millions of rows and thousands of queries per second when properly tuned — the defaults are conservative for safety, not speed. The single biggest win is enabling WAL mode (see [[sqlite/wal]]). After that, proper indexing and PRAGMA tuning are the next most impactful changes. Agents use this knowledge when databases are slow, queries take too long, or bulk operations need optimization.
body:
# SQLite Performance Tuning

SQLite's defaults prioritize safety and compatibility over speed. With proper configuration, SQLite can handle millions of rows and thousands of queries per second on modern hardware.

## The Performance PRAGMA Stack

Apply these PRAGMAs when opening every connection:

```sql
-- === MUST HAVE ===
PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL;            -- concurrent readers + writers (see [[sqlite/wal]])
PRAGMA synchronous = NORMAL;          -- safe with WAL, 5-10x faster writes
PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON;             -- enforce referential integrity
PRAGMA busy_timeout = 5000;           -- wait 5s on lock contention

-- === SHOULD HAVE ===
PRAGMA cache_size = -64000;           -- 64MB page cache (default is 2MB)
PRAGMA temp_store = MEMORY;           -- keep temp tables in RAM

-- === NICE TO HAVE ===
PRAGMA mmap_size = 268435456;         -- 256MB memory-mapped I/O
PRAGMA page_size = 4096;              -- default, good for most workloads
```

### Python Example

```python
import sqlite3

def open_db(path: str) -> sqlite3.Connection:
    conn = sqlite3.connect(path)
    conn.execute('PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL')
    conn.execute('PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL')
    conn.execute('PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON')
    conn.execute('PRAGMA busy_timeout=5000')
    conn.execute('PRAGMA cache_size=-64000')
    conn.execute('PRAGMA temp_store=MEMORY')
    conn.execute('PRAGMA mmap_size=268435456')
    return conn
```

### Node.js Example (better-sqlite3)

```javascript
import Database from 'better-sqlite3';

function openDb(path) {
  const db = new Database(path);
  db.pragma('journal_mode = WAL');
  db.pragma('synchronous = NORMAL');
  db.pragma('foreign_keys = ON');
  db.pragma('busy_timeout = 5000');
  db.pragma('cache_size = -64000');
  db.pragma('temp_store = MEMORY');
  db.pragma('mmap_size = 268435456');
  return db;
}
```

## PRAGMA Reference

| PRAGMA | Default | Recommended | Impact |
|--------|---------|-------------|--------|
| `journal_mode` | DELETE | WAL | Unlocks concurrent reads during writes |
| `synchronous` | FULL | NORMAL | 5-10x faster writes (safe with WAL) |
| `cache_size` | -2000 (2MB) | -64000 (64MB) | Fewer disk reads for repeated queries |
| `temp_store` | DEFAULT | MEMORY | Faster sorts, GROUP BY, DISTINCT |
| `mmap_size` | 0 | 268435456 (256MB) | Faster reads via memory-mapped I/O |
| `busy_timeout` | 0 | 5000 | Auto-retry on lock instead of failing |
| `page_size` | 4096 | 4096 | Rarely needs changing |
| `auto_vacuum` | NONE | INCREMENTAL | Reclaim space from deletes |

## EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN

Use `EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN` to understand how SQLite executes a query:

```sql
EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN
SELECT u.name, COUNT(o.id)
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id
WHERE u.active = 1
GROUP BY u.id;
```

Look for these in the output:

| Output | Meaning | Action |
|--------|---------|--------|
| `SCAN TABLE` | Full table scan | Add an index |
| `SEARCH TABLE ... USING INDEX` | Index lookup | Good |
| `USING COVERING INDEX` | Index-only query | Optimal |
| `USE TEMP B-TREE FOR ORDER BY` | Sorting without index | Add index matching ORDER BY |
| `USE TEMP B-TREE FOR GROUP BY` | Grouping without index | Add index matching GROUP BY |

### Index Design Rules

1. **Index columns in WHERE clauses** — `CREATE INDEX idx ON table(column)`
2. **Composite index order matters** — put equality conditions first, range conditions last
3. **Cover ORDER BY** — include ORDER BY columns at the end of composite indexes
4. **Partial indexes save space** — `CREATE INDEX idx ON users(email) WHERE active = 1`
5. **Covering indexes avoid table lookups** — include all SELECTed columns in the index
6. **Don't over-index** — each index slows down writes

```sql
-- BAD: Full table scan
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = 42 AND status = 'pending';

-- FIX: Composite index (equality columns first)
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_status ON orders(user_id, status);

-- BETTER: Covering index (avoids table lookup)
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_covering ON orders(user_id, status, total, created_at);
```

## Bulk Insert Optimization

Default: ~50 inserts/second. Optimized: ~100,000+ inserts/second.

### The Key: Transactions

Every INSERT outside a transaction is an implicit single-row transaction with a full fsync. Batch inserts in explicit transactions:

```sql
-- SLOW: 10,000 implicit transactions (one per row)
INSERT INTO logs (message) VALUES ('msg1');
INSERT INTO logs (message) VALUES ('msg2');
-- ... 10,000 times → ~50 rows/sec

-- FAST: 1 transaction for all rows
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO logs (message) VALUES ('msg1');
INSERT INTO logs (message) VALUES ('msg2');
-- ... 10,000 times → ~100,000+ rows/sec
COMMIT;
```

### Prepared Statements

```python
# Python — prepare once, execute many
conn.execute('BEGIN')
stmt = 'INSERT INTO logs (level, message, created_at) VALUES (?, ?, ?)'
conn.executemany(stmt, [
    ('INFO', 'started', '2026-04-11 12:00:00'),
    ('ERROR', 'failed', '2026-04-11 12:00:01'),
    # ... thousands more
])
conn.execute('COMMIT')
```

```javascript
// Node.js (better-sqlite3) — transaction helper
const insert = db.prepare('INSERT INTO logs (level, message) VALUES (?, ?)');
const insertMany = db.transaction((rows) => {
  for (const row of rows) insert.run(row.level, row.message);
});
insertMany(rows); // automatically wrapped in BEGIN/COMMIT
```

### Extreme Bulk Loading

For loading millions of rows (CSV import, initial data load):

```sql
PRAGMA synchronous = OFF;             -- DANGEROUS: no crash safety
PRAGMA journal_mode = MEMORY;         -- no journal file
PRAGMA cache_size = -256000;          -- 256MB cache
BEGIN;
-- ... bulk inserts ...
COMMIT;
-- RESTORE SAFE SETTINGS IMMEDIATELY
PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL;
PRAGMA synchronous = NORMAL;
```

## VACUUM and ANALYZE

### VACUUM

Rebuild the database file, reclaiming space from deleted rows:

```sql
-- Full vacuum (locks database, requires 2x disk space temporarily)
VACUUM;

-- Incremental vacuum (if auto_vacuum=INCREMENTAL)
PRAGMA incremental_vacuum(1000);      -- free up to 1000 pages
```

When to VACUUM:
- After deleting a large portion of data
- Database file is much larger than expected
- Want to defragment for sequential read performance

### ANALYZE

Collect statistics for the query planner:

```sql
-- Analyze all tables
ANALYZE;

-- Analyze specific table
ANALYZE users;

-- Let SQLite decide what needs analysis (recommended)
PRAGMA optimize;
```

Run `PRAGMA optimize` periodically (hourly/daily) and before closing long-lived connections.

## Common Performance Antipatterns

| Antipattern | Impact | Fix |
|-------------|--------|-----|
| No WAL mode | Readers block writers | `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL` |
| synchronous=FULL with WAL | Unnecessary fsync per commit | `PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL` |
| No busy_timeout | Immediate SQLITE_BUSY errors | `PRAGMA busy_timeout=5000` |
| Inserts without transaction | ~50 rows/sec vs ~100K/sec | Wrap in BEGIN/COMMIT |
| Missing indexes on WHERE columns | Full table scans | Add targeted indexes |
| SELECT * when only need 2 columns | Wasted I/O | Select only needed columns |
| No EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN | Blind optimization | Always check the plan |
| AUTOINCREMENT on primary key | Slight overhead, rarely needed | Use INTEGER PRIMARY KEY |
| String comparison without index | Full scan | Create expression index on lower() |
| LIKE '%pattern%' | Cannot use index | Use FTS5 (see [[sqlite/extensions]]) |

## Benchmarking

```bash
# Time a query from CLI
time sqlite3 myapp.db "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM large_table WHERE status = 'active';"

# SQLite built-in timer
sqlite3 myapp.db
.timer on
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM large_table WHERE status = 'active';
```

## Database Size Limits

| Limit | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Max database size | 281 TB |
| Max row size | 1 GB |
| Max columns per table | 2,000 |
| Max tables | No hard limit |
| Max SQL length | 1,000,000,000 bytes |
| Max attached databases | 125 |

sections.title: The performance PRAGMA stack
sections.body: Apply on every connection: journal_mode=WAL (concurrency), synchronous=NORMAL (fast writes), busy_timeout=5000 (lock retry), cache_size=-64000 (64MB cache), temp_store=MEMORY (fast temp ops), mmap_size=268435456 (256MB mmap). These are safe defaults for production. See [[sqlite/wal]] for WAL details.

sections.title: EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN
sections.body: Prefix any SELECT with EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN to see how SQLite will execute it. Look for SCAN TABLE (bad — needs index), SEARCH USING INDEX (good), USING COVERING INDEX (optimal). If you see USE TEMP B-TREE, add an index matching the ORDER BY or GROUP BY. Always check plans for queries on large tables.

sections.title: Indexing strategy
sections.body: Index columns in WHERE, JOIN ON, ORDER BY, GROUP BY. Composite index column order: equality conditions first, then range conditions, then ORDER BY. Partial indexes filter rows (WHERE clause on the index). Covering indexes include all columns the query selects, avoiding the table lookup. Expression indexes enable indexed lookups on lower(), json_extract(), etc. Don't over-index — each index adds write overhead. See [[sqlite/schema]] for CREATE INDEX syntax.

sections.title: Bulk insert optimization
sections.body: Individual inserts outside transactions: ~50 rows/sec (each is a full fsync). Batched in a transaction: ~100,000+ rows/sec. Always wrap bulk operations in BEGIN/COMMIT. Use prepared statements with executemany() (Python) or transaction() (better-sqlite3). For extreme loading, temporarily use synchronous=OFF and journal_mode=MEMORY, then restore safe settings immediately after.

sections.title: VACUUM and ANALYZE
sections.body: VACUUM rebuilds the database file, reclaiming space and defragmenting. Requires 2x disk space temporarily. Use PRAGMA auto_vacuum=INCREMENTAL and PRAGMA incremental_vacuum for lighter-weight space reclamation. ANALYZE collects statistics for the query planner. PRAGMA optimize runs ANALYZE only on tables that need it — call periodically (hourly/daily) and at connection close.

sections.title: Common performance antipatterns
sections.body: No WAL mode (readers block writers). synchronous=FULL with WAL (unnecessary overhead). No busy_timeout (instant SQLITE_BUSY failures). Inserts without explicit transactions (~50 vs ~100K rows/sec). Missing indexes on WHERE columns. SELECT * when only two columns needed. LIKE '%pattern%' instead of FTS5 ([[sqlite/extensions]]). AUTOINCREMENT when INTEGER PRIMARY KEY suffices.

links.context: WAL mode is the foundation of performance tuning
Index design is defined in the schema
Query patterns that benefit from tuning
FTS5 replaces slow LIKE patterns for full-text search

related.note: WAL mode is the single most impactful performance setting
Index design directly determines query performance
FTS5 solves the LIKE '%pattern%' performance problem

author: dojo-community
